blocks.content: By January, Ryan Callaway was a fact at the lunch table on Tuesdays and Thursdays.

He fit in the way he seemed to fit everywhere — without forcing it, without announcing himself. He asked questions and actually listened. He made Eli laugh twice in the first week, which was not nothing. Kit was polite.
Polite the way you are when the only other option is something you can't afford.

blocks.speaker: Ryan
blocks.content: Daisy said you grew up on the same block.

blocks.speaker: Ryan
blocks.content: She said you know every back road in the county.
Ryan tried again. Not pushily — the way someone does when they actually want to connect.

blocks.speaker: Ryan
blocks.content: My family moved around a lot. I never had a place I knew that well. A place that was just — mine, I guess.
Ryan looked at him. A pause.

blocks.speaker: Ryan
blocks.content: I'm just saying it sounds like something worth having.

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: It's just where I grew up.
He didn't leave. He just closed. Ryan absorbed it without making a thing of it — nodded once, turned toward Eli, let the conversation shift. He was good at that. He was good at a lot of things.

Daisy didn't say anything. But Kit felt her looking at him for the rest of lunch, reading him the way she always did. Ryan caught it too — the glance she gave Kit, and then the one she gave him, measuring something.
Eli saw the whole exchange. He didn't say a word at the table.
